summary: Justin Brennan teaches how to acquire 10-30 unit multifamily deals in 90 days with only $20,000 out of pocket by leveraging Other People's Money (OPM), eliminating three limiting beliefs: that you need significant capital, that multifamily is too complex, and that deals are hard to find. He demonstrates the deal-sourcing strategy using direct-to-broker and direct-to-owner approaches, with specific emphasis on accessing off-market deals through broker relationships.
takeaways: Target 10-30 unit deals for first investment (not 50+ units) because brokers and lenders expect newer investors at this size
Use 70% loan-to-cost financing on multifamily deals since banks finance the asset/business, not the person (need only 600+ credit score)
Only 30% of deals appear on Crexi/LoopNet; 70% sell in pre-market to 25-50 broker contacts—use public listings as 'lures' to meet brokers and access off-market deals
Raise capital in $25,000 minimum increments from 5-7 investors for 10-30 unit deals expecting 15-20% annual returns
Receive acquisition fee (2-3% of closing costs) paid within 7 days of closing—on $1.65M deal = ~$30,000 returned, reducing out-of-pocket to ~$20K
Get paid 5-8 ways in multifamily: cash flow, profits, acquisition fee, asset management fee (1% monthly revenue), construction management fee, selling fees, finder's fees
3-4 deals of 10-30 units typically generate $10,000-$20,000 monthly passive cash flow to leave traditional employment

skill_candidates.change: Shift from treating Crexi/LoopNet as deal sources to using them as broker discovery and relationship-building tools
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explains: 'When you go on Crexi, LoopNet, and CoStar, you're not going on there to find deals. You're going on there to find brokers. Flip the switch. That's what you use the websites for.' He shows Indiana example: 189 listings visible = only 30% of ~600 actual deals in market.

skill_candidates.change: Understand multifamily deal math requires analyzing 70% LTC loans, OPM equity splits, acquisition fees, and preferred returns rather than single-family metrics
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin walks through specific 15-unit example ($1.5M purchase + $100K renovations + $50K closing costs = $1.65M; 70% loan = $1.15M; 30% equity split 40/60 GP/LP) and shows acquisition fee mechanics: '$50K in, $30K fee back = $20K net out of pocket.'

skill_candidates.change: Reframe capital raising from 'asking people for money' to 'presenting deals to investors seeking 15-20% returns in real estate'
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin states: 'You're not asking for anything. You're giving them an opportunity...You just need a deal that makes financial sense to then attract the money in.' He emphasizes finding good deals matters most: 'The problem is not finding investors. The problem is finding a good deal that makes financial sense.'

title: How I Would Buy My First Apartment With Little Money!
